title: Best VPN for China in 2026: What Actually Bypasses the Great Firewall
description: The Great Firewall got a major upgrade in April 2026. Here's which VPNs still work in China, which obfuscation protocols survive GFW deep packet inspection, and what you need to set up before you land.
content:
## The April 2026 Crackdown Changed Everything

China's Great Firewall has always been aggressive, but April 2026 brought a qualitative shift. Chinese authorities conducted physical raids on data center infrastructure used by proxy and relay services across the country. The result: Shadowsocks, V2Ray, and Trojan-based services that tens of millions of users relied on were wiped out almost overnight. Many budget VPNs went dark. Even some established providers lost their relay infrastructure inside China and haven't recovered.

If you're traveling to China in 2026 and your VPN plan is "I'll figure it out when I get there," you're going to have a bad time. This guide covers what still works, why it works, and what you need to do before you board the plane.

## How the Great Firewall Actually Blocks VPNs

The GFW uses several layered techniques:

**IP blocking** is the oldest and crudest method. The firewall maintains blocklists of known VPN server IP addresses and simply drops connections to them. Most major VPN providers rotate IPs constantly to stay ahead of this.

**Deep packet inspection (DPI)** is far more sophisticated. Even on port 443 (standard HTTPS), the GFW can analyze traffic patterns and TLS fingerprints to identify VPN handshakes. WireGuard's distinctive UDP behavior, OpenVPN's certificate exchange, and even standard TLS have known signatures that GFW can flag.

**Protocol-level blocking** is what took down Shadowsocks at scale. Shadowsocks' AEAD encryption creates a detectable statistical fingerprint over time. The GFW probes suspected Shadowsocks endpoints and can verify them with active probing attacks.

**DNS poisoning** blocks domain lookups for VPN provider websites, which is why you often can't even download a VPN app once you're inside China.

## What Still Works in 2026

### TLS-Based Traffic Obfuscation

The VPNs that survive GFW today all use one approach: making their traffic look exactly like normal HTTPS web browsing at the packet level. This means:

- **uTLS fingerprint mimicry**: The VPN client copies the TLS ClientHello fingerprint of a real browser (Chrome, Firefox, Safari). The GFW can't distinguish it from browser traffic.
- **QUIC/HTTP3 tunneling**: WireGuard traffic wrapped inside what looks like HTTP/3 connections. Mullvad has implemented this specifically for high-censorship environments.
- **Domain fronting via CDN**: Routing traffic through major CDN providers (Cloudflare, Akamai, AWS) so that blocking the VPN means blocking the CDN, which China is reluctant to do.

### VPNs With Confirmed China Performance in 2026

**Astrill VPN** with its StealthVPN protocol is the most consistently cited option in ground-level testing from people actually in China. StealthVPN wraps OpenVPN in an SSL layer with randomized padding and browser-mimicking TLS. It's not the cheapest option, but it maintains relay infrastructure inside Asia specifically for Chinese connectivity.

**ExpressVPN** uses its Lightway protocol with obfuscation layered on top. Lightway is built on wolfSSL and uses a proprietary obfuscation layer that randomizes packet sizes and timing. It has a large server network in Hong Kong, Japan, and Singapore, which gives lower latency for users in eastern China.

**NordVPN** with obfuscated servers enabled works but requires manual server selection. NordVPN's obfuscated servers run OpenVPN over SSL with Obfsproxy-style traffic shaping. You need to explicitly switch to the obfuscated server list in settings, and you should pre-save several Asian server locations before you travel.

**Proton VPN** uses its Stealth protocol, which wraps WireGuard inside TLS 1.3 with a fake SNI header pointing to a legitimate domain. Proton has been more cautious about China performance claims but Stealth does work when standard protocols fail.

## The Legal Grey Area

This is important and worth being direct about. In China:

**For tourists and short-term visitors**: Using a VPN is technically illegal without government approval, but enforcement against foreign tourists is essentially nonexistent. You will not get arrested for using NordVPN on your phone. The law targets Chinese citizens and businesses operating unlicensed VPN infrastructure, not individuals browsing Instagram.

**For residents and long-term workers**: The risk profile is different. Chinese nationals face real penalties, and expats working for foreign companies exist in a grey zone. Most multinational companies operating in China use licensed "cross-border" network solutions rather than consumer VPNs for this reason.

**Approved VPNs exist**: China does allow companies to use VPNs that route through approved gateways. These are not privacy tools. They log everything and share data with authorities. Tourists don't need to worry about this distinction.

## What to Do Before You Land

This is the single most important piece of advice in this article: **install and test your VPN before your flight lands**.

Once you cross the border:
- VPN provider websites are blocked, so you can't sign up or download apps
- The App Store in China doesn't carry most VPN apps
- Google Play is unavailable entirely
- You can't receive an SMS verification if your home SIM isn't active

Pre-travel checklist:
1. Install at least two VPN apps from different providers (not just two subscriptions to the same company)
2. Enable obfuscation mode in each app's settings and confirm it's working
3. Download and cache several server locations in Asia (Hong Kong, Japan, Singapore, Taiwan)
4. Test with a kill switch enabled so you know what the app does when the VPN drops
5. Screenshot or write down the app's manual configuration settings in case you need to reinstall

## Our Recommendation

For most travelers, **NordVPN** covers the base case: obfuscated servers, a large Asian server network, apps on all platforms, and a kill switch. Enable obfuscated servers before you land and pre-select servers in Hong Kong and Japan.

If you're spending more than two weeks in China or you're doing anything sensitive, add **Astrill** as a backup. It's more expensive, but StealthVPN has the best track record for consistent performance through the GFW's DPI systems.

Don't rely on free VPNs. Most free services use shared infrastructure that gets blocked within hours of being identified. In China specifically, a VPN that fails at the wrong moment isn't just inconvenient, it cuts you off from maps, translation tools, and communication apps you depend on.

Expect speeds around 15-30 Mbps through any obfuscated VPN in China. Obfuscation overhead is real. If you need faster connections, connect to servers in Singapore or Taiwan rather than Hong Kong, where congestion is higher.
